role_hints.role: Creative staff (design, art direction, copy, production)
role_hints.oews_basis: 27-1011 Art Directors 4.1% of wage bill + 27-1024 Graphic Designers 3.3% + writers, producers and creative tail not in the top-10, est. ~8-12% more (role_mix is the 4-digit 5418 proxy)
role_hints.note: Production design, adaptations/versioning, copy drafts and asset generation are the most directly generative-AI-exposed work in the industry; creative direction, brand judgment and client sell-through persist. Plausible within-role fraction 0.4-0.6 for production tiers.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10 (a proxy artifact — account executives/supervisors are the largest real agency function); est. ~12-18% of wage bill
role_hints.note: Status decks, briefs, contact reports and coordination automate substantially; client relationship ownership is the industry's core asset and persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 6.6% + analytics tail est. ~3-5% more
role_hints.note: Research synthesis, media planning, campaign reporting and optimization are heavily AI-exposed — platforms automate the buying side natively.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 9.3% + 11-2021 Marketing Managers 5.2%
role_hints.note: Client and pitch leadership persists; internal reporting, resourcing and revenue management automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% + 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 3.0% — NOTE: much of this is contamination from sibling 5418 codes (media reps, display advertising); within true agencies this is new-business staff
role_hints.note: Pitch and RFP production automates heavily; rainmaking does not.

role_hints.oews_basis: 27-3031 Public Relations Specialists 8.3% — largely contamination from 541820 in the 4-digit mix; integrated agencies do carry comms/content staff
role_hints.note: Content and press material production automates; media relationships persist.

role_hints.role: Back office, admin and remaining tail
role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; finance, traffic/project management, HR, admin — 4-digit top-10 covers ~51% of wage bill; also excludes contaminant occupations 41-9011 Demonstrators 1.6% and 27-1026 Merchandise Displayers 1.2% (from 541850/541890, not agencies)
role_hints.note: Billing (client-cost reconciliation is notoriously manual), trafficking, timesheet and production admin — among the most automatable cost lines.

source_hints.why: Standard agency revenue rankings and industry commentary; recent editions track AI's effect on agency staffing and revenue models.

source_hints.source: 4A's (American Association of Advertising Agencies) research and benchmarking
source_hints.why: Trade-association data on agency operations, compensation-based fee structures and AI adoption among member agencies.

source_hints.source: ANA (Association of National Advertisers) agency-compensation and in-housing surveys
source_hints.why: Client-side evidence on fee structures (FTE-based fees dominate) and the in-housing trend — both sides of the capture question.
Deal flow and multiples for marketing-services transactions — active_consolidators and exit_mult.

source_hints.source: R3 agency M&A and new-business reviews
source_hints.why: Tracks agency acquisitions (holdcos, PE, consultancies) and account movement — consolidator identity and pace.

source_hints.source: COMvergence agency billings and pitch tracking
source_hints.why: Media-account movements and billings context; useful for how much agency revenue is media vs creative.

capture_questions: Agency fees are predominantly labor-based: FTE staffing plans, rate cards and retainers audited by client procurement — when AI cuts the hours behind a scope, do procurement-led fee renegotiations pass savings to clients (as they historically have), or can agencies shift to output/performance pricing and keep the spread? What fraction of industry revenue has actually moved off labor-based fees?

Who owns the client relationship — the agency (contracted AOR status, integration into client workflow) or individual leaders whose departures move accounts? What does account-movement data say about stickiness at the small-agency tier?

What stops clients from bypassing agencies entirely — in-housing plus platform automation (Google PMax, Meta Advantage+) plus generative creative tools — and what does the ANA in-housing trend data show at the SMB-client tier this code's small agencies serve?
Does principal-based media trading or proprietary data give any small-agency moat, or is that margin structurally reserved for holding companies?
terminal_value_question: No license protects advertising; the deciding mechanisms are platform disintermediation and in-housing. Ad platforms already generate creative and automate buying natively for advertisers — the question is whether the agency's product (judgment, brand stewardship, cross-platform orchestration) survives as a paid service when mature AI does creative and media execution end-to-end inside the platforms. Distinguish evidence that AI is cutting agency delivery costs (margin story) from evidence that advertisers are buying less agency service at all (product-replacement story) — headcount-per-revenue trends and account in-housing rates are the tell.
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate (central): FTE/rate-card-based fees with client procurement audits convert AI efficiency directly into client savings — the industry already lived this with procurement-driven fee compression; model in pi_dist explicitly.

Revenue accounting trap: gross billings (including media pass-through) vs net revenue — small-agency financials and quoted revenue multiples confuse the two constantly; normalize to net revenue before any multiple work. The record's labor_share 0.4814 is on an IPS output basis (HIGH quality) — receipts-based figures will look different.

EBITDA normalization: small-agency profit is SDE with owner comp inside; also project-revenue volatility and client-concentration (one lost account can halve EBITDA) — normalize and probe concentration.

Role-mix contamination: the 4-digit 5418 proxy includes demonstrators, merchandise displayers and media-rep sales agents from sibling codes (541830/541840/541850/541890) — do not build the agency V breakdown on those lines; use the adjusted structure above and say so.

Boundary confusion: digital/marketing agencies straddle 541810/541613/541511; media planning-buying is 541830; PR is 541820 — 'agency industry' surveys and multiples span all of them; check coverage before quoting.

Holding-company vs independent tiers: the top of this industry is already consolidated (WPP/Omnicom-IPG scale); the roll-up-relevant population is the 977-firm 20-99-employee class plus part of the 100-499 class per the record's n_band chain — use small-independent data, not holdco data, for B and M.
